content.problemStatement:
You are given an undirected weighted graph it has NNN vertices and MMM edges. 

You are tasked to find the distance of the shortest path from vertex 111 to all the other vertices.

Notice the larger constraints of this problem.

TaskSubmit the optimised and unoptimised approaches and checkout the verdicts.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a two integers NNN and MMM, denoting the number of vertices and edges.
Next MMM lines describes the edges. i−thi-thi−th of these MMM lines contains three space-separated integers uiu_iui​, viv_ivi​ and wiw_iwi​ denoting there exists an edge between uiu_iui​ and viv_ivi​ with weight wiw_iwi​.


Output Format
For each test case, output on NNN space-separated integers. Where the ithi^{th}ith integer denotes the distance from vertex 111 to vertex iii.


If a vertex is not reachable, print -1 instead.
constraints: The graph is simple (no self‑loops, no multiple edges).
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with two integers N and M denoting the number of vertices and edges. Then M lines follow, each containing three integers u, v and w describing an undirected edge between vertices u and v with weight w.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output N space‑separated integers on a single line. The i‑th integer is the shortest distance from vertex 1 to vertex i. If vertex i is unreachable print -1.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Shortest distances from vertex 1: to 1 is 0, to 2 is 3, to 3 is 2, to 4 is 3 (via 1‑3‑4), to 5 is 4 (via 1‑2‑3‑4‑5).

solution.approach: The optimal algorithm is Dijkstra’s shortest‑path algorithm using a min‑heap (priority queue). Because all edge weights are non‑negative, Dijkstra correctly computes the minimum distance from the source vertex 1 to every other vertex. We store the graph as an adjacency list to achieve O(N+M) memory. Each time we extract the vertex with the currently smallest tentative distance from the priority queue, we relax all its outgoing edges. If a better distance for a neighbour is found, we push the updated pair into the queue. Vertices are processed at most once (when they are popped the first time), guaranteeing the overall time O((N+M) log N) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O((N + M) log N) per test case
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N + M) for the adjacency list, distance array and priority queue
